verses.text: Hannah prayed, and said, My heart rejoiceth in the LORD, mine horn is exalted in the LORD: my mouth is enlarged over mine enemies; because I rejoice in thy salvation. There is none holy as the LORD: for there is none beside thee: neither is there any rock like our God. Guidance: Hannah's song. Three centuries before Mary will echo it (Luke 1:46-55), Hannah opens with the same theology: the LORD lifts the lowly and casts down the proud. The 'horn' is the strength-symbol of an animal — exalted means restored to dignity. 'There is no rock like our God' — God as immovable foundation, the same image Moses used in Deut 32. The song is a public theology, not a private feeling. The woman who could not speak above a whisper in chapter 1 now sings the year's most important psalm.

Hannah prayed, and said, My heart rejoiceth in the LORD, mine horn is exalted in the LORD: my mouth is enlarged over mine enemies; because I rejoice in thy salvation. There is none holy as the LORD: for there is none beside thee: neither is there any rock like our God. Guidance: Hannah's song. Three centuries before Mary will echo it (Luke 1:46-55), Hannah opens with the same theology: the LORD lifts the lowly and casts down the proud. The 'horn' is the strength-symbol of an animal — exalted means restored to dignity. 'There is no rock like our God' — God as immovable foundation, the same image Moses used in Deut 32. The song is a public theology, not a private feeling. The woman who could not speak above a whisper in chapter 1 now sings the year's most important psalm.

Talk no more so exceeding proudly; let not arrogancy come out of your mouth: for the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed. The bows of the mighty men are broken, and they that stumbled are girded with strength. They that were full have hired out themselves for bread; and they that were hungry ceased: so that the barren hath born seven; and she that hath many children is waxed feeble. Guidance: 'By him actions are weighed.' The LORD's invisible scale weighs every action of the strong and the comfortable. Four reversals: bows broken / stumblers strengthened; full ones working for bread / hungry filled; barren bears seven / many-childed wears out. Hannah names herself in code: 'the barren hath born seven' — she will go on to have five more children (v21), but seven is the symbol of completeness. Her single son Samuel will be more fruitful than Peninnah's brood. Sometimes one God-given child is worth a houseful of socially-engineered ones.

The LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up. The LORD maketh poor, and maketh rich: he bringeth low, and lifteth up. He raiseth up the poor out of the dust, and lifteth up the beggar from the dunghill, to set them among princes, and to make them inherit the throne of glory: for the pillars of the earth are the LORD's, and he hath set the world upon them. Guidance: The song's theological core. The LORD's sovereignty crosses every line we want to fence Him off from — death and life, wealth and poverty, the dunghill and the throne. He picks up beggars and seats them with princes. The world's foundations are His; He arranges the rooms. Hannah is not abstracting — she is herself a former 'beggar' (in spirit) now sitting among the princes of Israel's most important prayer-list. The personal testimony has flowered into national doctrine.

He will keep the feet of his saints, and the wicked shall be silent in darkness; for by strength shall no man prevail. The adversaries of the LORD shall be broken to pieces; out of heaven shall he thunder upon them: the LORD shall judge the ends of the earth; and he shall give strength unto his king, and exalt the horn of his anointed. Guidance: The song closes with the first prophecy of a king in scripture history. 'His king… his anointed (mashiach)' — Hebrew for Messiah. Centuries before any king is enthroned in Israel, Hannah's song already prays for one. The song that started as a barren woman's gratitude ends as the nation's first explicit messianic petition. The LORD who raised her out of the dust will raise up a king and anoint Him. Mary will quote it almost word for word over Jesus' cradle.

Elkanah went to Ramah to his house. And the child did minister unto the LORD before Eli the priest. Guidance: Elkanah leaves; the child stays. One sentence; the cost of the vow. The verb 'minister' (sharath) is what the chapter will repeat over Samuel — even as a small boy, he is doing priestly service. Eli the old priest will be the substitute father. The book's tension is set: a boy from Hannah's house growing up alongside Hophni and Phinehas in the same religious household, and the next paragraph is about to show why that home was no safe place.

Now the sons of Eli were sons of Belial; they knew not the LORD. The priests' custom with the people was, that, when any man offered sacrifice, the priest's servant came, while the flesh was in seething, with a fleshhook of three teeth in his hand; and he struck it into the pan, or kettle, or caldron, or pot; all that the fleshhook brought up the priest took for himself. So they did in Shiloh unto all the Israelites that came thither. Also before they burnt the fat, the priest's servant came, and said to the man that sacrificed, Give flesh to roast for the priest; for he will not have sodden flesh of thee, but raw. If any man said unto him, Let them not fail to burn the fat presently, and then take as much as thy soul desireth; then he would answer him, Nay; but thou shalt give it me now: and if not, I will take it by force. Wherefore the sin of the young men was very great before the LORD: for men abhorred the offering of the LORD. Guidance: 'They knew not the LORD' — at the door of His own sanctuary. The priests' sons commit two specific sins. First, theft of more than the legal priestly portion (Lev 7:31-35 gave the priests the breast and right thigh; they take whatever the hook spears). Second, demanding raw fat before God receives His portion in the burnt offering — a grosser violation, because the fat belonged exclusively to God (Lev 3:3-5, 17). The result: 'men abhorred the offering of the LORD.' Bad priests do not just hurt themselves; they keep worshipers away. The corruption of one generation's clergy will require the LORD's full housecleaning.

But Samuel ministered before the LORD, being a child, girded with a linen ephod. Moreover his mother made him a little coat, and brought it to him from year to year, when she came up with her husband to offer the yearly sacrifice. Eli blessed Elkanah and his wife, and said, The LORD give thee seed of this woman for the loan which is lent to the LORD. And they went unto their own home. The LORD visited Hannah, so that she conceived, and bare three sons and two daughters. And the child Samuel grew before the LORD. Guidance: A camera cut from the bad priests to the child in the linen ephod. The contrast is the chapter's whole rhetoric. Hannah sews a 'little coat' every year — the verb is tender, the garment grows with him. Once a year the family makes pilgrimage, and Hannah brings a new coat. Eli, knowing how rare such devotion is, blesses Elkanah and Hannah with more children — and the LORD does it. Three sons, two daughters. Hannah's 'seven' from her song (v5) is now fulfilled (Samuel + 5 more = 6, plus the original implied seventh as poetic completeness, or perhaps later children unmentioned). The LORD always supplies more than what was vowed away.

Now Eli was very old, and heard all that his sons did unto all Israel; and how they lay with the women that assembled at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. He said unto them, Why do ye such things? for I hear of your evil dealings by all this people. Nay, my sons; for it is no good report that I hear: ye make the LORD's people to transgress. If one man sin against another, the judge shall judge him: but if a man sin against the LORD, who shall intreat for him? Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto the voice of their father, because the LORD would slay them. Guidance: Eli is 'very old' and finally — finally — addresses the rumors. The second sin of his sons is now exposed: sexual abuse of the women who served at the sanctuary entrance (cf. Exod 38:8). Eli's rebuke is correct in theology ('who shall intreat for him?') but weak in execution — he asks, he reasons, he sighs. The narrator's verdict: 'they hearkened not, because the LORD would slay them.' Their hearts had hardened past hearing; God's judgment was already in motion. Some warnings come too late not because the speaker waited too long but because the hearer refused to listen even when they were spoken.

The child Samuel grew on, and was in favour both with the LORD, and also with men. Guidance: One verse, mirrored almost exactly in Luke 2:52 about the boy Jesus. Samuel grows on two axes simultaneously — vertical favor with God, horizontal favor with men. Compare the chapter's other son-track: Hophni and Phinehas grew on neither. Same household, same temple, same priestly clothes. The difference is the heart shaped by a mother's vow and a child's habit of ministering before the LORD. Generations to come will hear of both pairs of sons; only one will be remembered with longing.

There came a man of God unto Eli, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Did I plainly appear unto the house of thy father, when they were in Egypt in Pharaoh's house? Did I choose him out of all the tribes of Israel to be my priest, to offer upon mine altar, to burn incense, to wear an ephod before me? did I give unto the house of thy father all the offerings made by fire of the children of Israel? Wherefore kick ye at my sacrifice and at mine offering, which I have commanded in my habitation; and honourest thy sons above me, to make yourselves fat with the chiefest of all the offerings of Israel my people? Wherefore the LORD God of Israel saith, I said indeed that thy house, and the house of thy father, should walk before me for ever: but now the LORD saith, Be it far from me; for them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed. Guidance: An unnamed prophet — 'a man of God' — visits Eli with a heavy oracle. He rehearses the priestly grant: chosen out of Egypt, given altar, incense, ephod, and a portion of every offering. Then the indictment: 'thou honourest thy sons above me.' Eli's primary sin is not failing to discipline but loving his sons more than God. The prophet's verdict line is the book's permanent maxim: 'them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed.' The honor exchange is a two-way street. The hereditary high priesthood will be revoked from Eli's branch — not because God is fickle but because Eli put his sons before God's altar.

Did I choose him out of all the tribes of Israel to be my priest, to offer upon mine altar, to burn incense, to wear an ephod before me? did I give unto the house of thy father all the offerings made by fire of the children of Israel? Wherefore kick ye at my sacrifice and at mine offering, which I have commanded in my habitation; and honourest thy sons above me, to make yourselves fat with the chiefest of all the offerings of Israel my people? Wherefore the LORD God of Israel saith, I said indeed that thy house, and the house of thy father, should walk before me for ever: but now the LORD saith, Be it far from me; for them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed. Guidance: An unnamed prophet — 'a man of God' — visits Eli with a heavy oracle. He rehearses the priestly grant: chosen out of Egypt, given altar, incense, ephod, and a portion of every offering. Then the indictment: 'thou honourest thy sons above me.' Eli's primary sin is not failing to discipline but loving his sons more than God. The prophet's verdict line is the book's permanent maxim: 'them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed.' The honor exchange is a two-way street. The hereditary high priesthood will be revoked from Eli's branch — not because God is fickle but because Eli put his sons before God's altar.

There came a man of God unto Eli, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Did I plainly appear unto the house of thy father, when they were in Egypt in Pharaoh's house? Did I choose him out of all the tribes of Israel to be my priest, to offer upon mine altar, to burn incense, to wear an ephod before me? did I give unto the house of thy father all the offerings made by fire of the children of Israel? Wherefore kick ye at my sacrifice and at mine offering, which I have commanded in my habitation; and honourest thy sons above me, to make yourselves fat with the chiefest of all the offerings of Israel my people? Wherefore the LORD God of Israel saith, I said indeed that thy house, and the house of thy father, should walk before me for ever: but now the LORD saith, Be it far from me; for them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed. Guidance: An unnamed prophet — 'a man of God' — visits Eli with a heavy oracle. He rehearses the priestly grant: chosen out of Egypt, given altar, incense, ephod, and a portion of every offering. Then the indictment: 'thou honourest thy sons above me.' Eli's primary sin is not failing to discipline but loving his sons more than God. The prophet's verdict line is the book's permanent maxim: 'them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed.' The honor exchange is a two-way street. The hereditary high priesthood will be revoked from Eli's branch — not because God is fickle but because Eli put his sons before God's altar.

Behold, the days come, that I will cut off thine arm, and the arm of thy father's house, that there shall not be an old man in thine house. Thou shalt see an enemy in my habitation, in all the wealth which God shall give Israel: and there shall not be an old man in thine house for ever. The man of thine, whom I shall not cut off from mine altar, shall be to consume thine eyes, and to grieve thine heart: and all the increase of thine house shall die in the flower of their age. This shall be a sign unto thee, that shall come upon both thy sons, on Hophni and Phinehas; in one day they shall die both of them. Guidance: A four-fold curse: no more elderly in Eli's house, the family will watch the temple itself be reduced, the few survivors will be a grief, both sons will die on the same day. The 'sign' (v34) is precise enough to be verifiable: same day. Chapter 4 will fulfill it exactly — Hophni and Phinehas die in the same Philistine battle. God speaks first and acts second, so that when the act lands, no one can mistake who is acting. The prophecy is also pastoral mercy to Eli — he has time to repent. He will not.

I will raise me up a faithful priest, that shall do according to that which is in mine heart and in my mind: and I will build him a sure house; and he shall walk before mine anointed for ever. It shall come to pass, that every one that is left in thine house shall come and crouch to him for a piece of silver and a morsel of bread, and shall say, Put me, I pray thee, into one of the priests' offices, that I may eat a piece of bread. Guidance: Promise alongside curse. God will raise up a 'faithful priest' — fulfilled partially in Samuel (chapter 3 onward), more fully in Zadok in David's day (1 Kings 2), and ultimately in Christ the great High Priest. The phrase 'walk before mine anointed for ever' looks past every levite to the Messiah's eternal priesthood (Heb 7-9). And the dark mirror — Eli's remaining descendants will end up begging Zadok's house for crumbs of priestly income. The verse is the chapter's last word: God does not abandon the priesthood; He replaces the priests. Every dynasty in scripture that despises God's commands eventually finds itself the supplicant of a faithful successor. The chapter ends pointing past Samuel to Christ.
